elements.sent-2411-001: We present a systematic search for radio active galactic nuclei (AGNs) in dwarf galaxies using recent observations taken by the Very Large Array Sky Survey (VLASS).
elements.sent-2411-002: To select these objects, we first establish a criterion to identify radio-excess AGNs using the infrared-radio correlation (IRRC) parameter, $q$, that describes the tight relation between radio and IR emission in star forming (SF) galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-003: We find a $2\sigma$ threshold of $q < 1.94$ to select radio-excess AGNs, which is derived from a sample of $\sim 7,000$ galaxies across the full mass range in the NASA-Sloan Atlas (NSA) that have radio and IR detections from VLASS and the {\it Wide-Field Infrared Survey Explorer , respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-004: We create catalogs of radio-excess AGNs and SF galaxies and make these available to the community.
elements.sent-2411-005: Applying our criterion to dwarf galaxies with stellar masses $M_\star \lesssim 3 \times 10^9 M_\odot$ and redshifts $z \le 0.15$, and carefully removing interlopers, we find 10 radio-excess AGNs with radio-optical positional offsets between $\sim 0$ and $2\farcs3$ (0 - 2.7 kpc).
elements.sent-2411-006: Based on statistical arguments and emission line diagnostics, we expect the majority of these radio-excess AGNs to be associated with the dwarf host galaxies rather than background AGNs.
elements.sent-2411-007: Five of the objects have evidence for hosting AGNs at other wavelengths, and 5 objects are identified as AGNs in dwarf galaxies for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-008: The majority of black holes (BHs) belong to one of two populations: stellar mass BHs with masses $\sim10\:M_\odot$ and supermassive BHs (SMBHs) with masses $\sim10^{6-9 M_\odot$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: BHs with masses between these extremes are often referred to as intermediate mass BHs (IMBHs) and while hundreds of IMBHs with masses between $\sim10^{5-6 M_\odot$ have been discovered in dwarf galaxies (e.g., \citealt{reines2022 ), evidence for BHs with masses $\sim10^{2-5 M_\odot$ is scarce (\citealt{greene2020 and references therein).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In addition to their rarity, IMBHs are objects of interest because they are critical to better understanding the origin of SMBHs.
elements.sent-2411-011: For SMBHs to reach their immense sizes, it is widely accepted that they evolved from smaller BHs, known as seeds, that grew through accretion and mergers.
elements.sent-2411-012: Different seeding models have been proposed (e.g., \citealt{vol2008 ; \citealt{vol2009 ; \citealt{agarwal2012 ; \citealt{ricarte2018 ; \citealt{rose2022 ), but it remains unclear as to which models are the most accurate, or if a combination of models is closest to reality.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Each seeding model predicts a different size and quantity of seed BHs in the early universe, and consequently, predicts a different distribution of modern-day IMBHs.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, the discovery of more IMBHs may aid in constraining seeding models and improve our understanding of the way that SMBHs form and evolve.
elements.sent-2411-015: We study dwarf galaxies to optimize the likelihood of discovering IMBHs, since observational scaling relationships have shown that less massive galaxies host central BHs of lesser mass (e.g., \citealt{kor2013 ; \citealt{mcconnell2013 ; \citealt{reines2015 ; \citealt{bentz2018 ; \citealt{schutte2019 ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: According to these relations, the masses of the BHs near the center of dwarf galaxies lie within the IMBH mass range.
elements.sent-2411-017: AGNs in dwarf galaxies can be studied over a variety of wavelengths, and each wavelength regime has proven to have a unique set of advantages and disadvantages.
elements.sent-2411-018: In the optical regime, AGNs have been identified using narrow emission line diagnostics \citep{reines2013,moran2014, molina2021,salehirad2022 and by searching for broad H$\alpha$ emission (e.g., \citealt{greene2007 ; \citealt{dong2012 ; \citealt{reines2013 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The presence of broad H$\alpha$ in conjunction with AGN-like narrow line ratios has been shown to be effective in discerning between AGNs and supernovae (SNe), which can also produce broad H$\alpha$ lines in dwarf galaxies (\citealt{baldassare2016 ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: One limitation of optical diagnostics is that they typically can only find BHs that are radiating at large fractions of their Eddington luminosity, which is fairly uncommon.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, dust can extinguish optical light emitted from AGNs.
elements.sent-2411-022: X-ray emission is beneficial in detecting low-luminosity AGNs (e.g., \citealt{lemons2015 ; \citealt{reines2016 ; \citealt{mezcua2018 ; \citealt{latimer2019 ), but X-ray observations require large exposure times, which is not practical for large-scale searches.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Mid-IR observations also have the potential to identify AGNs, but when studying dwarf galaxies, it has proven difficult to separate the emission from intense star formation from the emission due to AGNs (\citealt{hainline2016 ; \citealt{satyapal2018 ; \citealt{latimer2021b ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Radio searches for AGNs have some advantages compared to other wavelength regimes.
elements.sent-2411-025: AGNs almost always create radio emission at centimeter wavelengths (\citealt{ho2008 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-026: Weaker radio signals from AGNs can come from a variety of sources, including AGN driven winds, thermal free-free emission from photoionized gas, and coronal activity from the accretion disc (see \citealt{panessa2019 , and references therein) .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Another advantage of using radio searches is that radio emission is nearly unaffected by interstellar dust extinction.
elements.sent-2411-028: For these reasons, previous radio searches have been used to identify AGNs in dwarf galaxies, using data collected by the Very Large Array \citep{mezcua2019, reines2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: One difficulty with working in the radio regime, however, is that radio emission is not unique to AGNs.
elements.sent-2411-030: Star formation (SF) creates radio emission that can mimic or mask the radio signals created by AGNs (e.g., \citealt{kimball2011 ; \citealt{condon2013 ; \citealt{zakamska2015 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: SF creates HII regions, which in turn produce thermal emission via free-free emission (e.g., \citealt{caplan1986 ), while SNRs and SNe create non-thermal radio emission via synchrotron radiation, as cosmic ray electrons from SNRs and SNe are accelerated in galactic magnetic fields (see the \citealt{condon1992 review).}}

elements.sent-2411-039: In this paper, we match the radio sources detected by VLASS to galaxies in the NASA-Sloan Atlas (NSA), similar to how \cite{reines2020 matched radio sources from the Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-centimeters (FIRST) Survey to the NSA.}

elements.sent-2411-371: We present a large-scale search for radio-excess AGNs in dwarf galaxies using data from the second epoch of VLASS.
elements.sent-2411-372: We first develop a criterion to distinguish between SF-related radio emission and AGN radio emission by analyzing the IRRC for $\sim 7,000$ galaxies in the NSA ($z \leq 0.15$) across the mass scale with VLASS and WISE detections (the NSA-VLASS-WISE sample).
elements.sent-2411-373: Using our derived $2\sigma$ threshold of $q < 1.94$, we find 723 galaxies with radio-excess AGNs within this NSA-VLASS-WISE sample.
elements.sent-2411-374: We have compiled catalogs of {6,904 radio-excess AGNs (including galaxies with low WISE S/N) and 6,049 SF-consistent galaxies in VLASS and make these available to the community (see Appendix).}
elements.sent-2411-375: Applying the criterion to bona fide dwarf galaxies, we find 10 that are apparent hosts of radio-excess AGNs.
elements.sent-2411-376: Our statistical analysis suggests that the majority of the radio-excess AGNs in our sample are indeed associated with the dwarf host galaxies, but we cannot completely rule out partial contamination from background AGNs.
elements.sent-2411-377: Our main results on the dwarf galaxy sample are summarized below:.
elements.sent-2411-378: The dwarf galaxies with radio-excess AGNs starkly differ from the majority of AGN hosts in the NSA-VLASS-WISE sample.
elements.sent-2411-379: The dwarf galaxy hosts have bluer colors and larger amounts of star formation than typical more massive radio-excess AGN hosts.
elements.sent-2411-380: Additionally, the dwarf galaxy hosts are not as centrally concentrated as the more massive hosts.
elements.sent-2411-381: This suggests that the dwarf hosts of radio-excess AGNs are similar to the general population of dwarf galaxies (e.g., Figure \ref{fig:dwarf_mass_gr ).}
elements.sent-2411-382: Five of the ten dwarf galaxies have evidence for hosting AGNs based on optical emission line diagnostic diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-383: Two of these also have mid-IR colors indicating the presence of an AGN.
elements.sent-2411-384: Importantly, the emission lines confirm the AGNs are associated with the dwarf galaxies and not background sources.
elements.sent-2411-385: Follow-up observations will be critical for discerning the cause of the variability of these sources.
elements.sent-2411-386: Five of the dwarf galaxies with radio-excess AGNs are uniquely identified as AGNs in this paper and are prime candidates for follow-up multi-wavelength observations.
elements.sent-2411-387: The radio-excess AGNs in the dwarf galaxies presented here are probable candidates for being IMBHs.
elements.sent-2411-388: If we assume the mass of the AGNs to follow the BH mass to total stellar mass ($M_{BH -M_{*}$) relation from \cite{reines2015 , we expect the BHs in these galaxies to have masses of $M\sim10^{4.9 -10^{5.8}\;M_\odot$, with a median value of $10^{5.7}\;M_\odot$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-389: We stress that there is a large amount of uncertainty in these estimates: the scatter in the \cite{reines2015 is quite large (0.55 dex).}
elements.sent-2411-390: This work shows the potential for finding new AGNs in dwarf galaxies using large-scale radio surveys.
elements.sent-2411-391: As such, upcoming radio facilities, such as the next generation VLA, could be utilized to efficiently find large numbers of radio-excess AGNs in dwarf galaxies (see \citealt{plotkin2018 ).}
elements.sent-2411-392: Such observations will better constrain the occupation fraction of IMBHs in dwarf galaxies, which will consequently constrain the seeding models for SMBHs.
